item.title: HDG-CLIP: Hierarchical Dual-Granularity Vision-Semantic Alignment for Open-Vocabulary Multi-Label Image Classification
item.dc:creator: Beiyan Liu
Sheng Huang
Bo Liu
Xiaobin Huang
Nankun Mu
Richang Hong
Meng Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Open-Vocabulary Multi-Label Image Classification (OV-MLIC) is an emerging task in computer vision aimed at recognizing unseen categories in real-world scenarios, leveraging Vision and Language Pre-training (VLP) models like CLIP. However, existing methods overlook the impact of category coupling and scale variation on cross-category knowledge transfer, thereby restricting performance on unseen categories. To address this issue, we propose a novel OV-MLIC method called Hierarchical Dual-Granularity Alignment-CLIP (HDG-CLIP), which emphasizes the complementary characteristics of different modalities and introduces a sample-category matching mechanism. Specifically, to address the category coupling issue, we construct semantic category prototypes to enhance cross-category knowledge transfer. Through the interaction between visual embeddings and category prototypes, we decouple category-specific information from mixed visual features and leverage the visual context of samples to learn category-level visual features. For mitigating the scale variation issue, we build a sample-category dual-granularity matching mechanism based on the difference in capture capability of different modalities across scales, thereby improving the object localization accuracy from a multi-dimensional perspective. Extensive experimental results show that HDG-CLIP exhibits state-of-art performance over existing methods on both the NUS-WIDE and the Open-Images datasets. Published: 2026-07-22T19:05:45+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.849 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Beiyan Liu; Sheng Huang; Bo Liu; Xiaobin Huang; Nankun Mu; Richang Hong; Meng Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3709489

Relevance: 0.849 (must_read)

Open-Vocabulary Multi-Label Image Classification (OV-MLIC) is an emerging task in computer vision aimed at recognizing unseen categories in real-world scenarios, leveraging Vision and Language Pre-training (VLP) models like CLIP. However, existing methods overlook the impact of category coupling and scale variation on cross-category knowledge transfer, thereby restricting performance on unseen categories. To address this issue, we propose a novel OV-MLIC method called Hierarchical Dual-Granularity Alignment-CLIP (HDG-CLIP), which emphasizes the complementary characteristics of different modalities and introduces a sample-category matching mechanism. Specifically, to address the category coupling issue, we construct semantic category prototypes to enhance cross-category knowledge transfer. Through the interaction between visual embeddings and category prototypes, we decouple category-specific information from mixed visual features and leverage the visual context of samples to learn category-level visual features. For mitigating the scale variation issue, we build a sample-category dual-granularity matching mechanism based on the difference in capture capability of different modalities across scales, thereby improving the object localization accuracy from a multi-dimensional perspective. Extensive experimental results show that HDG-CLIP exhibits state-of-art performance over existing methods on both the NUS-WIDE and the Open-Images datasets.

item.title: Multi-Level Feature Supervision through Mixup-Driven Hierarchical Self-Knowledge Distillation
item.dc:creator: Lei Zhao
Wing W. Y. Ng
Jianjun Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Conventional knowledge distillation (KD) depends on large teacher networks, adding computational overhead and limiting deployment flexibility. Teacher-free self-knowledge distillation (SKD) mitigates this issue, but existing methods typically apply uniform supervision. This approach overlooks the distinct functional roles and varying challenges present at different representational depths within modern hierarchical networks. To address this limitation, we introduce Hierarchical Self-Knowledge Distillation (HSKD), a framework designed to provide complementary, stage-specific supervision. HSKD integrates three coordinated components under the unifying operation of Mixup, each targeting a different level of network representation. First, shallow stage auxiliary reconstruction enhances feature richness and improves trainability where gradients are typically weakest. Second, deep stage contrastive learning promotes the acquisition of discriminative semantic representations where features are most abstract. Finally, output layer consistency regularization stabilizes decision boundaries to foster robust generalization. Together, these synergistic objectives create a knowledge refinement cascade from low-level features to high-level semantics, providing robust teacher-free guidance. Crucially, HSKD achieves this without increasing computational costs or architectural complexity during inference. Extensive experiments across various network backbones and benchmarks demonstrate that HSKD consistently matches or surpasses the performance of state-of-the-art SKD methods. Published: 2026-07-23T19:03:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.836 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lei Zhao; Wing W. Y. Ng; Jianjun Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3716467

Relevance: 0.836 (must_read)

Conventional knowledge distillation (KD) depends on large teacher networks, adding computational overhead and limiting deployment flexibility. Teacher-free self-knowledge distillation (SKD) mitigates this issue, but existing methods typically apply uniform supervision. This approach overlooks the distinct functional roles and varying challenges present at different representational depths within modern hierarchical networks. To address this limitation, we introduce Hierarchical Self-Knowledge Distillation (HSKD), a framework designed to provide complementary, stage-specific supervision. HSKD integrates three coordinated components under the unifying operation of Mixup, each targeting a different level of network representation. First, shallow stage auxiliary reconstruction enhances feature richness and improves trainability where gradients are typically weakest. Second, deep stage contrastive learning promotes the acquisition of discriminative semantic representations where features are most abstract. Finally, output layer consistency regularization stabilizes decision boundaries to foster robust generalization. Together, these synergistic objectives create a knowledge refinement cascade from low-level features to high-level semantics, providing robust teacher-free guidance. Crucially, HSKD achieves this without increasing computational costs or architectural complexity during inference. Extensive experiments across various network backbones and benchmarks demonstrate that HSKD consistently matches or surpasses the performance of state-of-the-art SKD methods.

item.title: Generative Retrieval for Unsupervised Text-Based Person Search
item.dc:creator: Mang Ye
Yucheng Ji
Yang Bai
Min Cao
Siyuan Chai
Bo Du
Min Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Text-based person search (TBPS) aims to retrieve images of a target person from a large image gallery based on a given natural language description. Most existing methods rely on supervised learning with manually annotated image-text pairs. In this paper, we explore unsupervised TBPS, with only unlabeled images. We propose GTR ^+ ^+ , a two-stage generation-then-retrieval framework. In the generation stage, we introduce a tiered description generation framework designed to produce fine-grained and stylistically diverse textual descriptions through a three-tier sequential process. The base tier leverages an automated question-and-answer mechanism to generate basic visual attribute descriptions; the intermediate tier enhances fine-grained detail using an inter-sample contrastive mechanism; the advanced tier further enriches textual diversity via a stylized expansion mechanism. In the retrieval stage, to mitigate the impact of noisy pseudo texts, we develop an adaptive confidence-weighted retrieval learning framework. We model image-text pairs as clean or noisy using a Gaussian Mixture Model, calibrated by real-time image-text similarity and static text generation probability from the prior stage, yielding adaptive sample weights during training. Beyond that, we also contribute LargeFine-Person, a large-scale TBPS dataset with high-quality, fine-grained, and diverse textual annotations, enabling a practical and generalizable TBPS pre-training benchmark under unsupervised setting. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:40+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.819 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mang Ye; Yucheng Ji; Yang Bai; Min Cao; Siyuan Chai; Bo Du; Min Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3713379

Relevance: 0.819 (must_read)

Text-based person search (TBPS) aims to retrieve images of a target person from a large image gallery based on a given natural language description. Most existing methods rely on supervised learning with manually annotated image-text pairs. In this paper, we explore unsupervised TBPS, with only unlabeled images. We propose GTR ^+ ^+ , a two-stage generation-then-retrieval framework. In the generation stage, we introduce a tiered description generation framework designed to produce fine-grained and stylistically diverse textual descriptions through a three-tier sequential process. The base tier leverages an automated question-and-answer mechanism to generate basic visual attribute descriptions; the intermediate tier enhances fine-grained detail using an inter-sample contrastive mechanism; the advanced tier further enriches textual diversity via a stylized expansion mechanism. In the retrieval stage, to mitigate the impact of noisy pseudo texts, we develop an adaptive confidence-weighted retrieval learning framework. We model image-text pairs as clean or noisy using a Gaussian Mixture Model, calibrated by real-time image-text similarity and static text generation probability from the prior stage, yielding adaptive sample weights during training. Beyond that, we also contribute LargeFine-Person, a large-scale TBPS dataset with high-quality, fine-grained, and diverse textual annotations, enabling a practical and generalizable TBPS pre-training benchmark under unsupervised setting.

item.title: Frequency-Spatial Dual-level Selective Alignment for Domain Adaptive Object Detection in Remote Sensing
item.dc:creator: Tingting Qiao
He Chen
Jue Wang
Aijia Shen
Wenchao Liu
Long Pang
Yizhuang Xie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing object detection suffers from severe performance degradation under cross-domain transfer, where domain gaps arise from differences in spectral response, spatial resolution, and viewing geometry. Most existing unsupervised domain adaptive object detection (DAOD) methods pursue cross-domain invariance through feature distribution alignment, but encounter two limitations specific to remote sensing: cross-domain appearance variation, where differences in imaging conditions produce divergent visual appearances, and foreground-background imbalance, where targets are sparsely distributed across vast backgrounds and alignment is dominated by background statistics. To address these two limitations, FS2A (Frequency-Spatial Dual-level Selective Alignment), a framework with two complementary modules, is proposed. At the frequency level, Scale-Class Conditioned Frequency Modulation (SCFM) decomposes multi-scale features via FFT and selectively modulates the low-frequency amplitude conditioned on scale level and categorical composition, restricting adversarial alignment to domain-variant spectral components while preserving the domain-invariant phase spectrum. At the spatial level, Kernel Relational Distillation (KRD) distills pairwise relational structure from a frozen satellite-pretrained vision foundation model in polynomial kernel space, where polynomial kernel functions preferentially concentrate alignment on foreground feature pairs over weakly correlated background pairs. Both modules are decoupled from the detection forward pass, introducing no additional inference cost. Experiments on two cross-domain remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that FS2A achieves 67.6% mAP50 on xView→DOTA, surpassing the state of the art by 2.7%, and competitive results on satellite-to-UAV benchmarks. The code will be available at https://github.com/sparklejojo/FSSA-DAOD. Published: 2026-07-23T19:03:01+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.811 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tingting Qiao; He Chen; Jue Wang; Aijia Shen; Wenchao Liu; Long Pang; Yizhuang Xie

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3716352

Relevance: 0.811 (must_read)

Remote sensing object detection suffers from severe performance degradation under cross-domain transfer, where domain gaps arise from differences in spectral response, spatial resolution, and viewing geometry. Most existing unsupervised domain adaptive object detection (DAOD) methods pursue cross-domain invariance through feature distribution alignment, but encounter two limitations specific to remote sensing: cross-domain appearance variation, where differences in imaging conditions produce divergent visual appearances, and foreground-background imbalance, where targets are sparsely distributed across vast backgrounds and alignment is dominated by background statistics. To address these two limitations, FS2A (Frequency-Spatial Dual-level Selective Alignment), a framework with two complementary modules, is proposed. At the frequency level, Scale-Class Conditioned Frequency Modulation (SCFM) decomposes multi-scale features via FFT and selectively modulates the low-frequency amplitude conditioned on scale level and categorical composition, restricting adversarial alignment to domain-variant spectral components while preserving the domain-invariant phase spectrum. At the spatial level, Kernel Relational Distillation (KRD) distills pairwise relational structure from a frozen satellite-pretrained vision foundation model in polynomial kernel space, where polynomial kernel functions preferentially concentrate alignment on foreground feature pairs over weakly correlated background pairs. Both modules are decoupled from the detection forward pass, introducing no additional inference cost. Experiments on two cross-domain remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that FS2A achieves 67.6% mAP50 on xView→DOTA, surpassing the state of the art by 2.7%, and competitive results on satellite-to-UAV benchmarks. The code will be available at https://github.com/sparklejojo/FSSA-DAOD.

item.title: TL-SGDR: Triple-Level Semantic-Geometric Decoupling and Refinement for Oriented Small Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yang Ning
Jiaxu Chen
Yanchao Bi
Liping Sun
Yinuo Li
Runhu Zhao
Xiankai Lu
Xiushan Nie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Oriented small object detection in remote sensing imagery faces the fundamental Semantic-Geometric Decoupling Dilemma (SGDD), where entangled feature representations cause semantic information to be overwhelmed by geometric background interference, parameter coupling in oriented bounding box regression, and severe sample imbalance.To resolve this core challenge, we propose a Semantic-Geometric Decoupling and Refinement (TL-SGDR) framework with three key innovations. First, our semantic-geometry decoupled feature pyramid network addresses feature-level SGDD by adaptively enhancing discriminative small-object To resolve this core challenge, we propose a Semantic-Geometric Decoupling and Refinement (TL-SGDR) framework with three key innovations. representations while suppressing background interference through contextaware fusion. Second, the semantic-customized prediction head eliminates parameter-level SGDD by constructing dedicated feature spaces for position, size, and orientation parameters. Third, a geometry-aware optimization strategy mitigates sample-level SGDD by dynamically selecting samples based on inherent size-precision relationships. Extensive experiments on four challenging benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, HRSC2016, and DIOR-R, demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving significant improvements of 1.73% mAP on DOTA-v1.5. Our work establishes a new paradigm for small object detection by fundamentally resolving the SGDD in remote sensing imagery. The code will be made publicly available at TL-SGDR. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:14+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yang Ning; Jiaxu Chen; Yanchao Bi; Liping Sun; Yinuo Li; Runhu Zhao; Xiankai Lu; Xiushan Nie

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3716509

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Oriented small object detection in remote sensing imagery faces the fundamental Semantic-Geometric Decoupling Dilemma (SGDD), where entangled feature representations cause semantic information to be overwhelmed by geometric background interference, parameter coupling in oriented bounding box regression, and severe sample imbalance.To resolve this core challenge, we propose a Semantic-Geometric Decoupling and Refinement (TL-SGDR) framework with three key innovations. First, our semantic-geometry decoupled feature pyramid network addresses feature-level SGDD by adaptively enhancing discriminative small-object To resolve this core challenge, we propose a Semantic-Geometric Decoupling and Refinement (TL-SGDR) framework with three key innovations. representations while suppressing background interference through contextaware fusion. Second, the semantic-customized prediction head eliminates parameter-level SGDD by constructing dedicated feature spaces for position, size, and orientation parameters. Third, a geometry-aware optimization strategy mitigates sample-level SGDD by dynamically selecting samples based on inherent size-precision relationships. Extensive experiments on four challenging benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, HRSC2016, and DIOR-R, demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving significant improvements of 1.73% mAP on DOTA-v1.5. Our work establishes a new paradigm for small object detection by fundamentally resolving the SGDD in remote sensing imagery. The code will be made publicly available at TL-SGDR.

item.title: Reorienting the Frozen Space: Training-Free Test-Time Adaptation by Geometric Transformation
item.dc:creator: Chenhao Ding
Jizhou Han
Songlin Dong
Xiang Song
Xinyuan Gao
Qiang Wang
Yuhang He
Zhiheng Ma
Yihong Gong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: With the widespread application of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in downstream tasks, test-time adaptation methods based on VLMs, particularly the training-free paradigm, have been gaining increasing attention due to their advantages in handling distribution shifts during testing. Yet, existing training-free methods remain constrained by the fixed geometry of pretrained feature spaces, which limits class separability. We propose SOBA, a training-free TTA method that edits decision geometry by re-expressing class prototypes in a test-induced orthogonal basis. SOBA maintains a lightweight dynamic queue of high-confidence test samples, derives an orthogonal basis via singular value decomposition, and aligns prototypes to the most discriminative directions of the test distribution. This simple adjustment enlarges inter-class margins, sharpens decision boundaries, and improves the recognition of semantically similar categories—without modifying features, prompts, or model parameters. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SOBA achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and superior efficiency compared to both training-free and backprop-based TTA methods. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenhao Ding; Jizhou Han; Songlin Dong; Xiang Song; Xinyuan Gao; Qiang Wang; Yuhang He; Zhiheng Ma; Yihong Gong

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3716498

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

With the widespread application of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in downstream tasks, test-time adaptation methods based on VLMs, particularly the training-free paradigm, have been gaining increasing attention due to their advantages in handling distribution shifts during testing. Yet, existing training-free methods remain constrained by the fixed geometry of pretrained feature spaces, which limits class separability. We propose SOBA, a training-free TTA method that edits decision geometry by re-expressing class prototypes in a test-induced orthogonal basis. SOBA maintains a lightweight dynamic queue of high-confidence test samples, derives an orthogonal basis via singular value decomposition, and aligns prototypes to the most discriminative directions of the test distribution. This simple adjustment enlarges inter-class margins, sharpens decision boundaries, and improves the recognition of semantically similar categories—without modifying features, prompts, or model parameters. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SOBA achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and superior efficiency compared to both training-free and backprop-based TTA methods.

item.title: RWSG: Region-Weighted Semantic Guidance for Pose-Free Sparse Multi-view Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Runqing Tan
Weiran Li
Shaoming Li
Heng Tong
Cheng Zhang
Fan Zhang
Junyu Dong
Qing Qing
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Pose-Free sparse 3D multi-view reconstruction plays a crucial role in computer vision, as it relies only on a limited number of views. Although existing methods have achieved significant progress, they depend solely on the restricted information available in the sparse views and often struggle to fully reconstruct the structure of objects. To address this challenge, we propose a Region-Weighted Semantic Guidance (RWSG) method for pose-free sparse 3D multi-view reconstruction, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction using only sparse views together with semantic information. Specifically, by fully leveraging semantics as contextual cues to weight the image information, our method guides the model to more effectively focus on object-relevant regions within the images—similar to how humans infer 3D structures from limited viewpoints based on real-world prior knowledge. Our method is primarily composed of two key components: Semantic Region Weighting (SRW) and the Cross-Modal Dual-Path Aggregator (CMDPA). First, we integrate the constructed semantic tokens with image tokens, allowing the image modality to incorporate semantic cues. To further optimize and refine semantic content while guiding the model to attend to meaningful structural information, the Semantic Region Weighting(SRW) converts the refined semantic embeddings into feature-level weighting signals. Finally, we introduce the Cross-Modal Dual-Path Aggregator (CMDPA) module to mitigate the inevitable noise introduced during the fusion of semantic and visual features. This module enhances feature representations through learnable tokens and selectively retains features that are more relevant to the object’s structure. Extensive experiments on the synthetic ShapeNet dataset and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art approaches across multiple evaluation metrics. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Runqing Tan; Weiran Li; Shaoming Li; Heng Tong; Cheng Zhang; Fan Zhang; Junyu Dong; Qing Qing

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3716507

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Pose-Free sparse 3D multi-view reconstruction plays a crucial role in computer vision, as it relies only on a limited number of views. Although existing methods have achieved significant progress, they depend solely on the restricted information available in the sparse views and often struggle to fully reconstruct the structure of objects. To address this challenge, we propose a Region-Weighted Semantic Guidance (RWSG) method for pose-free sparse 3D multi-view reconstruction, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction using only sparse views together with semantic information. Specifically, by fully leveraging semantics as contextual cues to weight the image information, our method guides the model to more effectively focus on object-relevant regions within the images—similar to how humans infer 3D structures from limited viewpoints based on real-world prior knowledge. Our method is primarily composed of two key components: Semantic Region Weighting (SRW) and the Cross-Modal Dual-Path Aggregator (CMDPA). First, we integrate the constructed semantic tokens with image tokens, allowing the image modality to incorporate semantic cues. To further optimize and refine semantic content while guiding the model to attend to meaningful structural information, the Semantic Region Weighting(SRW) converts the refined semantic embeddings into feature-level weighting signals. Finally, we introduce the Cross-Modal Dual-Path Aggregator (CMDPA) module to mitigate the inevitable noise introduced during the fusion of semantic and visual features. This module enhances feature representations through learnable tokens and selectively retains features that are more relevant to the object’s structure. Extensive experiments on the synthetic ShapeNet dataset and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art approaches across multiple evaluation metrics.

item.title: Condition-Enhanced Guided Diffusion for Image Super-Resolution
item.dc:creator: Jiati Cai
Yue Lei
Ke Wang
Ting Zhong
Fan Zhou
Jingkuan Song
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Diffusion models (DMs) have demonstrated significant promise in single-image super-resolution (SISR). However, the coarse introduction of low-resolution (LR) image conditions and the neglect of semantic-level cue mining limit the potential of DMs in super-resolution (SR) performance. We propose a Conditional Transition Sampling Strategy (CTS) to provide more content to the LR condition by progressively incorporating intermediate SR results without complex design of network modules or diffusion forms. To further overcome the constraint of intermediate SR result quality on the conditional guidance effect, we adopt Conditional Enhancement Training (CET) to strengthen the learning supervision of the prediction network and the perception of CTS. Furthermore, we develop a Dual-Branch Conditional Guidance Module (DCGM), which leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to extract semantic cues from both content restoration and degradation removal perspectives, thereby enhancing the model's semantic understanding and perceptual fidelity. Built upon CTS, CET, and DCGM, we propose COEN, a COndition-ENhanced Guided Diffusion Framework for SR. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of our proposed COEN, which consistently achieves superior SR performance. Published: 2026-07-22T19:05:27+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiati Cai; Yue Lei; Ke Wang; Ting Zhong; Fan Zhou; Jingkuan Song

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3716093

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Diffusion models (DMs) have demonstrated significant promise in single-image super-resolution (SISR). However, the coarse introduction of low-resolution (LR) image conditions and the neglect of semantic-level cue mining limit the potential of DMs in super-resolution (SR) performance. We propose a Conditional Transition Sampling Strategy (CTS) to provide more content to the LR condition by progressively incorporating intermediate SR results without complex design of network modules or diffusion forms. To further overcome the constraint of intermediate SR result quality on the conditional guidance effect, we adopt Conditional Enhancement Training (CET) to strengthen the learning supervision of the prediction network and the perception of CTS. Furthermore, we develop a Dual-Branch Conditional Guidance Module (DCGM), which leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to extract semantic cues from both content restoration and degradation removal perspectives, thereby enhancing the model's semantic understanding and perceptual fidelity. Built upon CTS, CET, and DCGM, we propose COEN, a COndition-ENhanced Guided Diffusion Framework for SR. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of our proposed COEN, which consistently achieves superior SR performance.

item.title: Pixel-Space Diffusion Transformers
item.dc:creator: Renye Yan
Jikang Cheng
You Wu
Ling Liang
Wei Peng
Athanasios V. Vasilakos
Qingyu Zhao
Yu Zhang
Yimao Cai
Kilian M. Pohl
Guoying Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient high-resolution image synthesis by denoising in a VAE-compressed latent space. However, fixed visual tokenizers can discard fine textures and structural details, while separate representation and diffusion training creates a mismatch between reconstruction and generation objectives. These limitations have renewed interest in pixel-space diffusion, which models raw pixels directly, removes the VAE bottleneck, and supports end-to-end optimization. This formulation better matches the demands of high-fidelity generation but introduces challenges in high-dimensional modeling, including noise scheduling, loss weighting, token efficiency, and scalable architecture design. Pixel-space modeling also offers a promising basis for unified multimodal systems: raw pixels, text, and task conditions can be represented in a shared token space and jointly processed by a single Transformer, narrowing the gap between visual understanding and generation. This paper reviews Pixel-Space Diffusion Transformers (pDiTs) from the perspectives of model architecture, continuous generative mechanisms, and unified multimodal modeling. We summarize representative methods, identify key technical challenges, and discuss future directions toward high-fidelity, end-to-end vision foundation models that integrate generation and understanding. Published: 2026-07-20T06:04:08+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Renye Yan; Jikang Cheng; You Wu; Ling Liang; Wei Peng; Athanasios V. Vasilakos; Qingyu Zhao; Yu Zhang; Yimao Cai; Kilian M. Pohl; Guoying Zhao

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient high-resolution image synthesis by denoising in a VAE-compressed latent space. However, fixed visual tokenizers can discard fine textures and structural details, while separate representation and diffusion training creates a mismatch between reconstruction and generation objectives. These limitations have renewed interest in pixel-space diffusion, which models raw pixels directly, removes the VAE bottleneck, and supports end-to-end optimization. This formulation better matches the demands of high-fidelity generation but introduces challenges in high-dimensional modeling, including noise scheduling, loss weighting, token efficiency, and scalable architecture design. Pixel-space modeling also offers a promising basis for unified multimodal systems: raw pixels, text, and task conditions can be represented in a shared token space and jointly processed by a single Transformer, narrowing the gap between visual understanding and generation. This paper reviews Pixel-Space Diffusion Transformers (pDiTs) from the perspectives of model architecture, continuous generative mechanisms, and unified multimodal modeling. We summarize representative methods, identify key technical challenges, and discuss future directions toward high-fidelity, end-to-end vision foundation models that integrate generation and understanding.

item.title: Lean-SAM2: Target-Anchored Memory and Encoder Acceleration for SAM2
item.dc:creator: Xudong Ouyang
Wenlun Zhang
Yimin Xu
Huazhong Liu
Yunshan Zhong
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: The Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) has advanced temporal promptable segmentation, yet its deployment remains hindered by heavy memory cross-attention overhead and redundant full-frame visual feature extraction. While recent methods explore efficiency via heuristic memory pruning and window-based sparse routing, they typically suffer from catastrophic performance degradation in complex segmentation scenarios replete with occlusions and distractors. To resolve these limitations, we propose \textbf{Lean-SAM2}, a holistic lightweight framework designed to address the above vulnerabilities while systematically eliminating computational redundancies. Specifically, Lean-SAM2 integrates three collaborative mechanisms: (1) Target-Anchored Memory Pruning (TAMP) safeguards target tokens against deceptive attention by modulating raw attention significance with semantic consistency against prompt-derived foreground anchors; (2) Temporal Condensation with Insurance Memory (TCIM) condenses historical context via a visibility-gated fusion while conditionally archiving high-confidence entries in a parallel insurance bank; and (3) Target-Anchored Risk-Aware Routing (TARR) selectively activates the heavy image encoder for target-related windows based on anchor similarity, utilizing a risk-aware fallback policy to trigger full-frame refreshes during volatile transitions. Extensive evaluations across multiple challenging benchmarks demonstrate that Lean-SAM2 establishes a superior balance between accuracy and efficiency. For example, on the LVOSv2 validation dataset, Lean-SAM2 achieves overall inference speedups of $1.412\times$ and $1.417\times$ on the SAM2.1-Large and SAM2.1-Base+, respectively, significantly outperforming Efficient-SAM2 while boosting the corresponding $\mathcal{J}\&\mathcal{F}$ scores by $5.0\%$ and $3.6\%$. Code is available at https://github.com/DeawhaleQwQ/Lean-SAM2. Published: 2026-07-22T06:42:27+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xudong Ouyang; Wenlun Zhang; Yimin Xu; Huazhong Liu; Yunshan Zhong

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

The Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) has advanced temporal promptable segmentation, yet its deployment remains hindered by heavy memory cross-attention overhead and redundant full-frame visual feature extraction. While recent methods explore efficiency via heuristic memory pruning and window-based sparse routing, they typically suffer from catastrophic performance degradation in complex segmentation scenarios replete with occlusions and distractors. To resolve these limitations, we propose \textbf{Lean-SAM2}, a holistic lightweight framework designed to address the above vulnerabilities while systematically eliminating computational redundancies. Specifically, Lean-SAM2 integrates three collaborative mechanisms: (1) Target-Anchored Memory Pruning (TAMP) safeguards target tokens against deceptive attention by modulating raw attention significance with semantic consistency against prompt-derived foreground anchors; (2) Temporal Condensation with Insurance Memory (TCIM) condenses historical context via a visibility-gated fusion while conditionally archiving high-confidence entries in a parallel insurance bank; and (3) Target-Anchored Risk-Aware Routing (TARR) selectively activates the heavy image encoder for target-related windows based on anchor similarity, utilizing a risk-aware fallback policy to trigger full-frame refreshes during volatile transitions. Extensive evaluations across multiple challenging benchmarks demonstrate that Lean-SAM2 establishes a superior balance between accuracy and efficiency. For example, on the LVOSv2 validation dataset, Lean-SAM2 achieves overall inference speedups of $1.412\times$ and $1.417\times$ on the SAM2.1-Large and SAM2.1-Base+, respectively, significantly outperforming Efficient-SAM2 while boosting the corresponding $\mathcal{J}\&\mathcal{F}$ scores by $5.0\%$ and $3.6\%$. Code is available at https://github.com/DeawhaleQwQ/Lean-SAM2.

item.title: Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network for Hyperspectral Salient Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yanyan Peng
Tingfa Xu
Yao Xiao
Peifu Liu
Shuyan Bai
Fengxiang Xu
Jianan Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Hyperspectral salient object detection aims to identify visually salient regions from hyperspectral images. Existing methods often fail because they fundamentally misunderstand the data, confusing incidental spectral variations caused by external factors such as illumination with essential spectral differences caused by the intrinsic material properties of the object. This leads to fragile representations and noisy predictions. To this end, we propose a lightweight and efficient Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network (S3GNet), with structure perception as the core, to build a closed-loop information flow around spectrum robust modeling, cross-stream co-perception and multi-scale refinement decoding. S3GNet introduces a parameter-free Spectral Structure-Aware Module that leverages spectral derivatives and regional hierarchical modeling to extract intrinsic features of robustness against illumination variations. Our Stream-Aware Attention Module achieves effective spectral-spatial collaboration through inter-stream global interaction and intra-stream spatial guidance. Furthermore, a Progressive Gated Refinement Decoder ensures precise object boundaries and detail recovery by optimally integrating multi-scale features. Experimental results show that S3GNet achieves superior performance in both computational efficiency and detection accuracy compared to existing methods. Published: 2026-07-22T19:08:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanyan Peng; Tingfa Xu; Yao Xiao; Peifu Liu; Shuyan Bai; Fengxiang Xu; Jianan Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3716067

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Hyperspectral salient object detection aims to identify visually salient regions from hyperspectral images. Existing methods often fail because they fundamentally misunderstand the data, confusing incidental spectral variations caused by external factors such as illumination with essential spectral differences caused by the intrinsic material properties of the object. This leads to fragile representations and noisy predictions. To this end, we propose a lightweight and efficient Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network (S3GNet), with structure perception as the core, to build a closed-loop information flow around spectrum robust modeling, cross-stream co-perception and multi-scale refinement decoding. S3GNet introduces a parameter-free Spectral Structure-Aware Module that leverages spectral derivatives and regional hierarchical modeling to extract intrinsic features of robustness against illumination variations. Our Stream-Aware Attention Module achieves effective spectral-spatial collaboration through inter-stream global interaction and intra-stream spatial guidance. Furthermore, a Progressive Gated Refinement Decoder ensures precise object boundaries and detail recovery by optimally integrating multi-scale features. Experimental results show that S3GNet achieves superior performance in both computational efficiency and detection accuracy compared to existing methods.

item.title: DTP-Track: Diversity-Guided Token Pruning and Purification for Vision-Language Tracking
item.dc:creator: Geng Xiong
Bineng Zhong
Qihua Liang
Xiaohai Li
Ning Li
Shuxiang Song
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Cross-modal interaction between natural language descriptions and visual features is essential for vision-language (VL) tracking. However, mainstream VL tracking methods typically fuse language with the full set of visual tokens, which leads to an unbalanced interaction paradigm where linguistic cues are easily overwhelmed by excessive visual information. This issue weakens semantic guidance and degrades tracking performance. To address this problem, we propose DTP-Track, a VL tracking framework that improves cross-modal interaction through dynamic token selection and feature purification. Specifically, we design a Diversity-based Visual Token Pruning (DVP) module that selects representative and diverse visual tokens for subsequent interaction with language, instead of directly performing dense fusion over the entire visual token set. In addition, we introduce a Cross-modal Feature Purification (CFP) module to further refine the fused representation by suppressing noisy alignments and preserving reliable semantic cues. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks, including LaSOT, LaSOText, OTB99-Lang, and TNL2K, demonstrate that DTP-Track achieves strong and competitive performance, and outperforms previous VL trackers on most evaluated benchmarks. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:01+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Geng Xiong; Bineng Zhong; Qihua Liang; Xiaohai Li; Ning Li; Shuxiang Song

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3716451

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Cross-modal interaction between natural language descriptions and visual features is essential for vision-language (VL) tracking. However, mainstream VL tracking methods typically fuse language with the full set of visual tokens, which leads to an unbalanced interaction paradigm where linguistic cues are easily overwhelmed by excessive visual information. This issue weakens semantic guidance and degrades tracking performance. To address this problem, we propose DTP-Track, a VL tracking framework that improves cross-modal interaction through dynamic token selection and feature purification. Specifically, we design a Diversity-based Visual Token Pruning (DVP) module that selects representative and diverse visual tokens for subsequent interaction with language, instead of directly performing dense fusion over the entire visual token set. In addition, we introduce a Cross-modal Feature Purification (CFP) module to further refine the fused representation by suppressing noisy alignments and preserving reliable semantic cues. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks, including LaSOT, LaSOText, OTB99-Lang, and TNL2K, demonstrate that DTP-Track achieves strong and competitive performance, and outperforms previous VL trackers on most evaluated benchmarks.

item.title: SAR-Inspired Adversarial Diffusion for Optical-to-SAR Image Translation
item.dc:creator: Xianshu Ao
Shiqi Xing
Sinong Quan
Haoyu Zhang
Xinyuan Su
Penghui Ji
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Generating synthetic SAR data from optical images is an effective way to alleviate the shortage of SAR samples and support data augmentation for remote sensing interpretation. Due to the distinct imaging mechanisms of optical and SAR sensors, optical-to-SAR translation still faces challenges such as conditioning collapse, cross-modal semantic inconsistency, and insufficient physical constraints. To address these issues, we propose SAR-Inspired Adversarial Diffusion (SIAD) for optical to-SAR image translation. First, we design a Swin-UNETR generator that spatializes the timestep and injects multi-scale temporal information into each encoding stage, preserving stage aware information in hierarchical features. Second, we introduce a conditional discriminator to enhance semantic consistency between the generated SAR image and the input optical image. Third, we incorporate a SAR-inspired regularization term to improve SAR fidelity and promote structural and speckle-statistical consistency. Qualitative results show that SIAD generates natural textures and SAR-consistent speckle statistics in homogeneous areas, while preserving clear boundaries and coherent spatial structures in heterogeneous areas. Experiments on three public datasets covering six typical land-cover types show that, compared with the strongest baseline BBDM, SIAD achieves relative improvements of 32.4% in PSNR and 22.3% in SSIM, while reducing the average raw KL divergence by approximately 73.8%. These results suggest that SIAD can generate high quality, SAR-domain-consistent synthetic SAR data for data augmentation and downstream interpretation tasks. Published: 2026-07-23T19:06:29+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xianshu Ao; Shiqi Xing; Sinong Quan; Haoyu Zhang; Xinyuan Su; Penghui Ji

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715849

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Generating synthetic SAR data from optical images is an effective way to alleviate the shortage of SAR samples and support data augmentation for remote sensing interpretation. Due to the distinct imaging mechanisms of optical and SAR sensors, optical-to-SAR translation still faces challenges such as conditioning collapse, cross-modal semantic inconsistency, and insufficient physical constraints. To address these issues, we propose SAR-Inspired Adversarial Diffusion (SIAD) for optical to-SAR image translation. First, we design a Swin-UNETR generator that spatializes the timestep and injects multi-scale temporal information into each encoding stage, preserving stage aware information in hierarchical features. Second, we introduce a conditional discriminator to enhance semantic consistency between the generated SAR image and the input optical image. Third, we incorporate a SAR-inspired regularization term to improve SAR fidelity and promote structural and speckle-statistical consistency. Qualitative results show that SIAD generates natural textures and SAR-consistent speckle statistics in homogeneous areas, while preserving clear boundaries and coherent spatial structures in heterogeneous areas. Experiments on three public datasets covering six typical land-cover types show that, compared with the strongest baseline BBDM, SIAD achieves relative improvements of 32.4% in PSNR and 22.3% in SSIM, while reducing the average raw KL divergence by approximately 73.8%. These results suggest that SIAD can generate high quality, SAR-domain-consistent synthetic SAR data for data augmentation and downstream interpretation tasks.

item.title: Collaborative Optimization of Receptive Field and Texture Preservation for Remote Sensing Small Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Sainan Zhou
Qi Qi
Jianhui Wu
Wei He
Siyuan Chen
Bing Tu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Small object detection in remote sensing imagery remains a challenging task due to severe scale variations, weak texture characteristics, and complex background interference. Existing methods often fail to effectively balance receptive field expansion and texture preservation, resulting in degraded feature representations for small objects. To address this issue, we propose a collaborative optimization framework, termed the Receptive Field-Texture Preservation Network (RTP-Net), which enhances small object detection through three complementary modules: the Global-Local Feature Extraction Module (GLEM), the Adaptive Weighted Enhancement Module (AWEM), and the Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAF). In particular, GLEM jointly models global contextual information and fine-grained local textures to alleviate feature degradation caused by scale variation. AWEM further enhances texture preservation and enhances discriminative capability via adaptive feature reweighting. In addition, MSAF improves multi-scale feature fusion by incorporating attention mechanisms, which enhance feature consistency while suppressing background interference and reducing misclassification in complex scenes.Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art detectors, particularly in small object detection tasks. Notably, RTP-Net achieves 84.1% mAP on the DIOR dataset, 95.3% mAP on the NWPU VHR-10 dataset, and 40.3% mAP on the AI-TOD dataset. These results validate the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed framework across diverse datasets and varying object scales. Published: 2026-07-22T19:04:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sainan Zhou; Qi Qi; Jianhui Wu; Wei He; Siyuan Chen; Bing Tu

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715676

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Small object detection in remote sensing imagery remains a challenging task due to severe scale variations, weak texture characteristics, and complex background interference. Existing methods often fail to effectively balance receptive field expansion and texture preservation, resulting in degraded feature representations for small objects. To address this issue, we propose a collaborative optimization framework, termed the Receptive Field-Texture Preservation Network (RTP-Net), which enhances small object detection through three complementary modules: the Global-Local Feature Extraction Module (GLEM), the Adaptive Weighted Enhancement Module (AWEM), and the Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAF). In particular, GLEM jointly models global contextual information and fine-grained local textures to alleviate feature degradation caused by scale variation. AWEM further enhances texture preservation and enhances discriminative capability via adaptive feature reweighting. In addition, MSAF improves multi-scale feature fusion by incorporating attention mechanisms, which enhance feature consistency while suppressing background interference and reducing misclassification in complex scenes.Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art detectors, particularly in small object detection tasks. Notably, RTP-Net achieves 84.1% mAP on the DIOR dataset, 95.3% mAP on the NWPU VHR-10 dataset, and 40.3% mAP on the AI-TOD dataset. These results validate the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed framework across diverse datasets and varying object scales.

item.title: Diffusion-Driven Multi-View Depth Estimation with Epistemic and Morphological Priors for Urban Scene Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Jin Liu
Binfeng Zou
Zhao Huang
Guangyuan Zhang
Haibing Yin
Zhiwen Zheng
Xiaoshuai Zhang
Xingru Huang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Urban scene reconstruction plays a critical role in applications such as autonomous navigation and digital twin systems. As a key technique for scene reconstruction, Multi-View Stereo (MVS) has made significant progress in general scenarios. However, it remains fragile in complex urban environments. Challenges such as weak textures, dense occlusion, depth adhesions, and illumination variations severely impair the effectiveness of traditional photometric and geometric consistency constraints, leading to unstable depth estimation and topological artifacts. To address these issues, we propose StableMVS, a robust depth estimation method that integrates semantic priors, structural cues, and perturbation-aware learning. Specifically, Epistemic-Structural Prior Infusion introduces cognitive priors and semantic layout from large vision models to enhance depth inference in ambiguous regions. Morphology-Encoded Depth Field Optimization leverages boundary-aware morphology to suppress distortions and topological artifacts near depth discontinuities. Furthermore, Diffusion-Propelled Feature Fidelity Learning adopts a disturbance–restoration paradigm that improves feature stability under real-world perturbations such as lighting shifts and sensor noise. These components collectively form a unified framework that bridges global understanding with local consistency, yielding structurally coherent and resilient depth estimation across challenging urban scenarios. Experiments on multiple public datasets show that StableMVS achieves competitive accuracy under low-texture, occlusion, and illumination variation conditions, validating its effectiveness for real-world MVS applications. The code is available at https://github.com/IMOP-lab/StableMVS. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.797 (must_read)

Authors: Jin Liu; Binfeng Zou; Zhao Huang; Guangyuan Zhang; Haibing Yin; Zhiwen Zheng; Xiaoshuai Zhang; Xingru Huang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3716258

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Urban scene reconstruction plays a critical role in applications such as autonomous navigation and digital twin systems. As a key technique for scene reconstruction, Multi-View Stereo (MVS) has made significant progress in general scenarios. However, it remains fragile in complex urban environments. Challenges such as weak textures, dense occlusion, depth adhesions, and illumination variations severely impair the effectiveness of traditional photometric and geometric consistency constraints, leading to unstable depth estimation and topological artifacts. To address these issues, we propose StableMVS, a robust depth estimation method that integrates semantic priors, structural cues, and perturbation-aware learning. Specifically, Epistemic-Structural Prior Infusion introduces cognitive priors and semantic layout from large vision models to enhance depth inference in ambiguous regions. Morphology-Encoded Depth Field Optimization leverages boundary-aware morphology to suppress distortions and topological artifacts near depth discontinuities. Furthermore, Diffusion-Propelled Feature Fidelity Learning adopts a disturbance–restoration paradigm that improves feature stability under real-world perturbations such as lighting shifts and sensor noise. These components collectively form a unified framework that bridges global understanding with local consistency, yielding structurally coherent and resilient depth estimation across challenging urban scenarios. Experiments on multiple public datasets show that StableMVS achieves competitive accuracy under low-texture, occlusion, and illumination variation conditions, validating its effectiveness for real-world MVS applications. The code is available at https://github.com/IMOP-lab/StableMVS.

item.title: TinyShareNet: Towards Extremely Tiny Network for Memory Efficient On-Chip Inference at the Edge
item.dc:creator: Tong Wu
Juntao Guan
Rui Lai
Changchun Chai
Zhangming Zhu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: The recent progress in TinyML technologies facilitates the deployment of machine learning on low-cost edge devices strictly constrained by computation, memory and energy. However, the extremely limited on-chip memory makes it hard to balance efficiency and performance during the inference phase. To address this issue, we propose a memory-efficient framework termed TinyShareNet that enables convolution groups with minimal parameters to achieve powerful performance. Motivated by sparse dictionary learning, we present an innovative linear convolution sparse representation (LCSR) paradigm to construct linear convolution groups for improving the expressive ability. On this basis, we propose a novel parameter sharing strategy across stacked linear convolution groups for significantly shrinking the parameter size. For further promoting the precision of parameter sharing tiny network, we put forward an adaptive orthogonality regularization (AOR) method on shared basis convolutions to learn more diverse features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed TinyShareNet achieves outstanding precision compared to state-of-the-art lightweight networks with a record-low storage cost. When deployed on popular microcontroller units (MCU), TinyShareNet yields significantly higher precision with over 1.56× higher frame rate and 1.88× lower energy consumption than classical solutions, greatly promoting the development of on-chip deep learning inference at the edge. Published: 2026-07-23T19:02:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.795 (must_read)

The recent progress in TinyML technologies facilitates the deployment of machine learning on low-cost edge devices strictly constrained by computation, memory and energy. However, the extremely limited on-chip memory makes it hard to balance efficiency and performance during the inference phase. To address this issue, we propose a memory-efficient framework termed TinyShareNet that enables convolution groups with minimal parameters to achieve powerful performance. Motivated by sparse dictionary learning, we present an innovative linear convolution sparse representation (LCSR) paradigm to construct linear convolution groups for improving the expressive ability. On this basis, we propose a novel parameter sharing strategy across stacked linear convolution groups for significantly shrinking the parameter size. For further promoting the precision of parameter sharing tiny network, we put forward an adaptive orthogonality regularization (AOR) method on shared basis convolutions to learn more diverse features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed TinyShareNet achieves outstanding precision compared to state-of-the-art lightweight networks with a record-low storage cost. When deployed on popular microcontroller units (MCU), TinyShareNet yields significantly higher precision with over 1.56× higher frame rate and 1.88× lower energy consumption than classical solutions, greatly promoting the development of on-chip deep learning inference at the edge.

item.title: GeoAlign: Foundation Model-driven Asymmetric Dual-Stream Manifold Alignment for Unsupervised Cross-View Geo-Localization
item.dc:creator: Juzheng Liu
Hanlin Qin
Xupei Zhang
Zibo Pang
Chenguang Deng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) is critical for autonomous drone navigation in GNSS-denied environments. Existing supervised frameworks rely on costly paired annotations, hindering open-world deployments. Unsupervised alternatives mitigate data dependency but struggle with severe non-linear geometric distortions, specifically perspective disparities and scale inconsistencies from drone altitude and attitude variations. Optimization instability can also arise from noisy pseudo-labels. Addressing these challenges, we present GeoAlign, a novel asymmetric dual-stream unsupervised framework. Observing that conventional symmetric architectures struggle to handle complex non-linear geometric distortions inherent in drone views, GeoAlign incorporates an Asymmetric Geometry-Rectified Adapter (AGRA). This module employs a Mixture-of-Experts exclusively on the drone branch, rectifying distorted manifolds towards the standard satellite feature space. To ensure robust convergence without ground-truth supervision, we introduce an Optimal Transport-driven Curriculum Alignment (OTCA) mechanism that uses the Sinkhorn algorithm to generate consistent soft pseudo-labels, which subsequently guide the optimization via bidirectional soft distillation and reliability-driven complex matching. Furthermore, the system integrates an Intrinsic Structure Mining Stream (ISMS) to mine compact intrinsic structures. By employing manifold-constrained whitening to enforce statistical independence, this stream eliminates channel redundancy and uncovers fine-grained discriminative cues. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that GeoAlign achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive unsupervised performance. By freezing the foundation backbone and adapting cached descriptors, GeoAlign reduces repeated image-level training cost while remaining competitive with several supervised methods. This work validates the efficacy of integrating distinct geometric priors into vision foundation models for robust g... Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) is critical for autonomous drone navigation in GNSS-denied environments. Existing supervised frameworks rely on costly paired annotations, hindering open-world deployments. Unsupervised alternatives mitigate data dependency but struggle with severe non-linear geometric distortions, specifically perspective disparities and scale inconsistencies from drone altitude and attitude variations. Optimization instability can also arise from noisy pseudo-labels. Addressing these challenges, we present GeoAlign, a novel asymmetric dual-stream unsupervised framework. Observing that conventional symmetric architectures struggle to handle complex non-linear geometric distortions inherent in drone views, GeoAlign incorporates an Asymmetric Geometry-Rectified Adapter (AGRA). This module employs a Mixture-of-Experts exclusively on the drone branch, rectifying distorted manifolds towards the standard satellite feature space. To ensure robust convergence without ground-truth supervision, we introduce an Optimal Transport-driven Curriculum Alignment (OTCA) mechanism that uses the Sinkhorn algorithm to generate consistent soft pseudo-labels, which subsequently guide the optimization via bidirectional soft distillation and reliability-driven complex matching. Furthermore, the system integrates an Intrinsic Structure Mining Stream (ISMS) to mine compact intrinsic structures. By employing manifold-constrained whitening to enforce statistical independence, this stream eliminates channel redundancy and uncovers fine-grained discriminative cues. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that GeoAlign achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive unsupervised performance. By freezing the foundation backbone and adapting cached descriptors, GeoAlign reduces repeated image-level training cost while remaining competitive with several supervised methods. item.title: CMDiff-Reg: Geometry-Enhanced Diffusion Intermediate Representations for Unsupervised Cross-Modality Remote Sensing Registration
item.dc:creator: Xiaochen Wei
Weiwei Guo
Wenxian Yu
Feiming Wei
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Cross-modality image registration in remote sensing remains a challenging problem due to the large radiometric discrepancies across heterogeneous sensors and the lack of precisely aligned training annotations. Existing unsupervised approaches typically rely on indirect supervision based on feature similarity or image translation. However, similarity-based methods often fail to accurately capture true spatial displacement under severe modality gaps, while translation-based strategies may suppress geometric structures or introduce artifacts due to the absence of reliable supervision. To address these challenges, we propose CMDiff-Reg, an unsupervised cross-modality remote sensing registration framework based on diffusion-denoised intermediate representations (DDIR). Instead of explicitly translating images across modalities, CMDiff-Reg introduces diffusion-generated intermediate representations that serve as a auxiliary modality for self-supervised learning. Specifically, we design a geometry-enhanced conditional diffusion model that incorporates displacement-sensitive geometric conditions into the denoising process. The diffusion model is trained with precise noise supervision, enabling the generated representations to preserve geometric structures while introducing significant appearance variations across modalities. By leveraging these geometry-consistent yet appearance-diverse representations, CMDiff-Reg constructs challenging self-supervised cross-modal training pairs with known geometric transformations, allowing the registration network to directly learn displacement estimation under large modality discrepancies. This design effectively avoids unstable pseudo supervision in translation-based frameworks and improves training stability and representation robustness. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-modality benchmarks demonstrate that CMDiff-Reg consistently outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised registration methods and achieves performance competitive... Published: 2026-07-23T19:03:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.794 (must_read)

Existing unsupervised approaches typically rely on indirect supervision based on feature similarity or image translation. However, similarity-based methods often fail to accurately capture true spatial displacement under severe modality gaps, while translation-based strategies may suppress geometric structures or introduce artifacts due to the absence of reliable supervision. To address these challenges, we propose CMDiff-Reg, an unsupervised cross-modality remote sensing registration framework based on diffusion-denoised intermediate representations (DDIR). Instead of explicitly translating images across modalities, CMDiff-Reg introduces diffusion-generated intermediate representations that serve as a auxiliary modality for self-supervised learning. Specifically, we design a geometry-enhanced conditional diffusion model that incorporates displacement-sensitive geometric conditions into the denoising process. The diffusion model is trained with precise noise supervision, enabling the generated representations to preserve geometric structures while introducing significant appearance variations across modalities.
